story:
Lowriders dust the curb outside La Mala Vida, where Rudi cleans out a stack of Marines on the back room's dim pool table. Before he can pocket the cash and slip out, the latch slams and fists fly. He wakes in a hospital, eye swollen, ribs screaming. His sister Madeline, a nurse, slides him pain pills and a warning. She'd already pulled a baggie of coke from his pocket before the cops could. Rudi says this job—real money, one last score—is his way out. She hugs him hard enough to make him yelp and makes him promise he won't come back under a sheet. He shrugs on his red-and-white flannel and walks out.

Out in the desert, Boon crushes a Coors can and listens to his ex-wife lay it out: their little girl needs a doctor, and if he won't find the cash, she's leaving Texas. He knows she's right. He fishes out a plain white card—Friday, 10 a.m.—and smokes in the quiet until there's nothing left to do but go.

At a noisy yard office that smells like diesel and cigar smoke, the self-proclaimed "King of El Paso," Salazar Garcia, lays out the job. A large shipment of his cocaine was hijacked by a small biker crew near Vegas. Retrieve it. Boon bristles at working with the kid; Salazar calls in a favor. After Rudi's sent to the Jeep, Salazar leans in: "Take the kid, and make sure he doesn't come back." Boon nods; he's a soldier. Outside, he tosses Rudi the keys. "You're driving."

They ride an hour in silence until the tank kisses E. At a lonely gas stop, Rudi jogs inside with a twenty while Boon lifts a wool blanket in the trunk and reveals an armory. He loads a Colt, straps on a shoulder rig. When they're rolling again, the desert air blows the smell of gun oil out the windows. Rudi talks—how this score is his ticket out, how broke they were after crossing the border. Boon smokes and keeps his eyes forward. "Get out before you can't," he says.

Near midnight, they park off the highway outside a sagging saloon on the outskirts of Rancho Charleston. The plan's simple: Boon slips in back and hunts for the case; Rudi makes noise at the front. "No gun for you," Boon says. Rudi nods—and palms a snub-nosed .38 when Boon turns away.

Out back, a biker staggers into the dark to piss. Boon moves fast, knife to the neck, and lays the body down in his own warm puddle. Inside, Rudi plays drunk and harmless. "This is a private club, muchacho." He shrugs and slumps by the door, stalling. In a closed office, Boon spots the briefcase and another biker. A flash: a shot to the throat. Then chaos. Boon bursts out, the 1911 barking, drops three men, dives behind the bar, buries his knife between the ribs of a fourth. The last biker draws on him—then goes pale and falls. Rudi stands with the .38, barrel smoking.

Boon lifts the case. "Is that it?" Rudi asks. "This is it," Boon says. "Good job, ki—"

The shot hammers the room. Boon looks down at the blooming red on his shirt and collapses. Rudi staggers, then kneels, flips the latches. White powder, bricks of it. He digs the Jeep keys from his own pocket and stands. A cold hand clamps his wrist. "Leave this life," Boon rasps, and his head sinks to the dusty floorboards.
